topic: rock art conservation — feral animals damaging sites

turns.content: What do you know about protecting rock art sites from feral animal damage. Buffaloes mainly but pigs too.

Feral animals can do serious damage to rock art sites in a few ways -- buffalo rubbing against rock surfaces, pigs rooting around the base of shelters and destabilising the soil, and both animals causing erosion that exposes or undermines painted surfaces. What kind of damage are you dealing with?

All of that. I found buffalo rub marks on a shelter wall last week. Not on the art itself but close, maybe half a metre away. The ground around the drip line of another shelter has been dug up by pigs. They are after roots and grubs in the soft soil under the overhang. Once that soil goes the rock face can crack and flake.

Half a metre from the art is way too close. For the buffalo, the most effective approach is physical barriers -- rock bollards or fencing that keeps them away from the shelter walls without being visually intrusive. Some sites in the Top End have used low stone walls or strategically placed logs to redirect animal movement.

For the pigs, it's harder because they're more persistent. Is there a feral pig control program in the area, or is it just you managing it?

There is a control program but it is not enough. They do aerial culls in the wet season when the pigs congregate on high ground but the numbers come back. And the pigs are smart, they learn the patterns. The shelter sites are like a restaurant for them, soft ground, grubs, cool shade. They keep coming back to the same spots.

That's the frustrating cycle with feral pigs -- you knock the numbers down and they breed back up within a season. For the specific art sites, you might need site-level protection rather than relying on broad-scale control. Some options:

- **Wire mesh fencing** around the most vulnerable shelters, buried at the base so pigs can't root under it
- **Motion-activated deterrents** like lights or sound devices, though these need maintenance and power
- **Trap corrals** near the shelters to catch repeat offenders

Have you had any luck with fencing individual sites before?

We have fenced a couple of the big public sites, the ones tourists visit. Those are maintained properly. But there are hundreds of art sites out here that nobody sees except us rangers. You cannot fence them all. The budget is not there and the land is too rough to get materials in to most of them.

That's the real challenge -- scale. Hundreds of sites across rough country with limited budget. For the unfenced sites, it might come down to prioritising. Which sites are at highest risk and which have the most significant art? A risk matrix based on:

- Evidence of recent animal activity
- Proximity to water or shade that attracts animals
- Significance and condition of the art

That way you're directing your limited resources to where they'll have the most impact. Are you documenting the damage when you find it?

I photograph everything. Every patrol I check the sites on my sector and photograph any changes. We have a database going back years now. Some sites are getting worse every dry season. The pigs come in hard when the wet season pools dry up because the ground around the shelters stays damp longer.

That photographic record is valuable -- it gives you evidence of the rate of deterioration and can support funding applications. The seasonal pattern you're describing is useful too. If you know pigs hit certain shelters hardest as pools dry up, you could time interventions around that -- even temporary barriers or increased trapping effort at those sites during the transition period between wet and dry.

Temporary barriers is an interesting idea. Something I can carry in and set up then remove later. Because permanent fencing on remote sites, it degrades too. The termites get into wooden posts, the wire rusts. Then you have a broken fence and an art site with rubbish around it which is worse than nothing.

Exactly -- degraded fencing becomes its own conservation problem. For temporary barriers, you could look at portable electric fencing with a solar-powered energiser. Lightweight enough to carry in, effective against both pigs and buffalo, and you can move it between sites based on seasonal risk. Some wildlife management programs use them for exactly this kind of rotational site protection.

Solar electric fence could work for some of the more accessible sites. The ones deep in the escarpment country, nothing is getting carried in there easily. Those sites you just have to monitor and hope the culling keeps numbers down enough.

For those remote escarpment sites, documentation might be the most realistic approach right now -- keep building that photographic evidence so when funding does come through, you can show exactly which sites need intervention first. Have you had any conversations with heritage or conservation bodies about grants specifically for rock art protection?

There have been some talks with the heritage people. They come out, they look, they agree it is bad, they write a report. Then it goes quiet. The funding cycle is slow. Meanwhile the pigs do not wait for a grant application to be approved. That is the part that is hard to sit with. These paintings are thousands of years old and a pig can damage them in one night.

That contrast is stark -- thousands of years of art versus one night of damage. The bureaucratic timeline and the ecological timeline just don't match. Keep pushing the documentation angle and building the case. The records you're creating now might be what tips a funding decision eventually. And in the meantime, the portable electric fencing for the accessible high-risk sites could at least protect the ones you can reach.
That is all I can do. Protect what I can reach and keep the records for the rest. I will look into the portable fencing options. Thanks for the ideas.
Glad to help. The work you're doing documenting and monitoring those sites matters, even when the system moves slowly. Good luck with the fencing and with getting more resources.
